mappings.rationaleAI: Covers propositional and first-order classical logic, modal, higher-order, and deviant logics, fallacies, metalogic, and formal semantics — directly spanning the entire formal logic (A), metalogic (B), and applied logic (C) outline. The most comprehensive single-article match for this section.

Gödel's incompleteness theorems are the defining results on completeness and consistency of formal systems (B.3) and his work on the independence of the axiom of choice addresses decidability; his contributions to modal logic (clarifying classical, intuitionistic, modal relations) also maps to A.4 and B.4.

Set theory and natural-number arithmetic constitute outline path A.5 directly; ZFC axiomatization, cardinality, and Cantor's results are the core content of formal set theory as a branch of the predicate calculus.

AI employs formal logic — automated theorem proving, constraint satisfaction, planning systems — as core techniques; these correspond to logics of physical application (C.4) and practical logic dealing with planning and decision-making (C.3). Applied formal logic is foundational to AI reasoning systems.

Mathematical proof is the direct subject of proof theory within metalogic (B.1 — nature and elements of metalogic); questions of completeness, consistency, and the limits of formal provability (B.3) are central to its treatment of formal and informal proof methods.

Aristotle's syllogistic is the historical origin of the theory of the syllogism (A.3) and his Organon established the predicate logic framework (A.2); he is the foundational figure for formal logic's development of both propositional and predicate analysis.

The semantic theory of truth and Tarski's metalanguage analysis bear directly on model theory (B.5 — interpretations that satisfy axioms of a formal system) and the liar paradox connects to formal fallacies (C.1.b). Addresses logical semantics and truth-value gaps relevant to metalogic.

Addresses critique of forms of reasoning — deductive, inductive, abductive — including systematic biases and logical fallacies (C.1.b), making it relevant to the applied logic section's treatment of argumentation theory and analysis of fallacious reasoning (C.1).

Formal ontology — including Quine's criterion of ontological commitment based on existential quantification — is part of model theory and the study of what entities formal systems are committed to (B.5); directly connects the predicate calculus's quantifiers to ontological questions.

Modal logic — outline path A.4 — concerns necessity, possibility, and contingency, which are the central concepts of metaphysical modality; the article on metaphysics directly addresses necessary vs. contingent truths and possible worlds, which modal logic formally systematizes.

Epistemic logic (C.2) deals with the logical analysis of belief, knowledge, justification, and assertion — the precise subject matter of epistemology. Foundationalism, coherentism, and the logic of knowledge are shared concerns.

Turing's resolution of Hilbert's Entscheidungsproblem established undecidability of the predicate calculus (B.3, B.4 — decidability and undecidability of logical calculi); the Turing machine is foundational to the theory of computability within metalogic.

Variables — including bound and free variables in mathematical logic — are the core syntactic elements of the predicate calculus (A.2.a), where individual variables and predicate variables with universal and existential quantifiers are defined. This article addresses their role in formal logical language.

Natural number arithmetic formally defined via Peano axioms connects directly to A.5 (set theory and natural-number arithmetic as branches of formal logic); the foundations of arithmetic and its axiomatic treatment are core content of this outline path.

Cantor's theory of infinite cardinalities and the axiom of infinity in ZFC are directly part of A.5 (set theory); the question of whether infinite formal systems can be proven consistent is central to B.3 (completeness, consistency, Gödel's results).

The laws of thought (identity, non-contradiction, excluded middle) are discussed in this article and are the traditional axioms of classical logic; the article's treatment of counterfactual thinking connects to modal logic applications, and the logic of judgment relates to epistemic logic (C.2).

The scientific method employs deductive and inductive logic, and its philosophical tensions (empiricism vs. rationalism, falsifiability) relate to C.1 (critique of forms of reasoning) and C.4 (logics of physical application — logic applied to empirical inference and experimental design).

Modern cryptography rests on mathematical logic and computational complexity — public-key cryptography depends on number-theoretic hardness assumptions and is a prime application of formal logical and mathematical methods to physical/digital systems (C.4 — logics of physical application).

Shannon information theory provides a logic of encoding, transmission, and entropy — foundational to the logics of physical application (C.4) and connecting formal logical structure to computational and communication systems.

Mathematics covers mathematical logic, set theory, and the foundational crisis (axiomatic method, proof theory), which overlap with A.5 (set theory and natural-number arithmetic in formal logic) and broadly with metalogic (B); the distinction between logic and mathematics is itself a metalogical question.
